text: As a result, the study deals with the issues of Polish–Moldovan relations in the period from 1492 to 1538. The starting point is established as the accession to the Polish throne of John Alexander, while the end point as the fall of prince Peter Rareş and Moldova's increased subordination to the Ottomans, which led to an end of the conflict between the two powers over Pokuttya. The direct border between Poland and the Ottoman Empire was also established after the Turks had conquered Budjak. The author presents the relations of Moldova with the Polish throne of each of the successive rulers: John Alexander, Alexander, and finally Sigismund I the Old, the basis for which she uses, among others, the previously unknown sources referring to the Levoča congress which was to prepare the planned expedition of John Alexander to Moldova in 1497, and the function of the Chodecki family in the struggles over Pokuttya during the reigns of the last two rulers, as well as the policy of prince Peter Rareş, which is an attempt to assess the actions of the prince in relation to his main neighbours: Poland, Hungary and Türkiye. As a result of an extremely wide-ranging research into various sources including, among others, the archives and libraries in Germany, Austria, Romania, Moldova, Hungary, Poland and Russia, the study presents the results of the author's research into the state of Polish–Moldovan relations during this period, providing new answers to the issues raised. The necessity of making a more thorough geopolitical analysis of the mutual relations between Poland and Moldova in the 16th century was caused by the role played by Moldova as a crossroads of the interests of various countries and their alliances including Poland, Hungary, Türkiye and the Grand Duchy of Moscow. The adequate explanation of the mutual relations between Poland and Moldova therefore required an analysis of the geopolitical situation and the political conditions of both countries.
